sourceRef.citation: Kourovka Notebook, Problem 18.94 (Khukhro & Mazurov eds., ed.18)

statement.latex: Let$G$be a group without involutions,$a$an element of it that is not a square of any element of$G$, and$n$an odd positive integer. Is it true that the quotient$G/ (a^{n})^{G}$does not contain involutions? { A.\,I.\,Sozutov} No, not always, as shown by an example of V.\,I.\,Trofimov; another example: the quotient of$G= a,b a^2=b^2$by$a^G$has order 2.

reiAssessment.solveRationale: Group theory VI_BRIDGING; Rei not specialized in group theory (yet)
reiAssessment.formalizationNotes: Kourovka problems are typically research-level group theory
